question: A team is structuring the test suite for an invoice-approval process. According to the docs' divide-and-conquer guidance, how should the happy path and the exceptional/fork paths be organized into test methods?

options.text: Cover the happy path in one consistent (big) test method, then test each fork/error path as its own separate test method.
Combine the happy path and every exceptional path into one giant test method for full end-to-end coverage.
Test only the happy path with automated tests; leave fork and error paths to integration testing later.
Split the happy path across many tiny test methods so that each BPMN element has its own assertion.

a.text: The docs say to fully test the happy path in one (big) test method to keep the data flow consistent and readable, then test forks/detours and errors/exceptional paths as chunks in separate test methods.

b.text: A single giant test method conflates many scenarios, hides failures, and is the opposite of the docs' divide-and-conquer recommendation.

c.text: The docs explicitly catalogue exceptional paths (rejection, timeout auto-approval, archive system error) as automated test methods, not deferred work.

d.text: The happy path is meant to live in one consistent method to clarify the default data flow — fragmenting it loses the readability that's the whole point.

explanation: The docs' divide-and-conquer guidance is: one big test for the happy path (so the default scenario is easy to read and onboard with), then a separate test method per fork or error path (rejection, timeout auto-approval, archive system error). That gives meaningful units without overspecifying.
